results.possibleIdentity: Guy Bishop (born Gunter Brueg; 1926–2009) was a German-born American scientific journalist and industrial executive active in the plastics and polymers industry from the 1950s through the 1970s. After emigrating to the United States in 1952 and graduating from the Columbia School of Journalism, he began his career as an editor for Modern Plastics magazine, where he served as the Midwestern editor in the late 1950s. He later transitioned into the corporate sector, becoming a Vice President at General Foam, a division of Tenneco Chemicals Incorporated based in New York. His technical expertise encompassed plastics, polymers, elastomers, and various foam technologies (including urethane foam and potentially honeycomb structures). Bishop was also recognized as a gifted artist and photographer, a background that aligns with his association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which fostered collaborations between the artistic and technical communities in New York during the late 1960s. Before his career in the United States, he was a decorated World War II British Intelligence Major who participated in the capture of Heinrich Himmler.
